text:
*[Bug 18997] New: Allow print method for auto-printed object to see original object name*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18997>

            Bug ID: 18997
           Summary: Allow print method for auto-printed object to see
                    original object name
           Product: R
           Version: R 4.5.x
          Hardware: All
                OS: All
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: enhancement
          Priority: P5
         Component: Wishlist
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:dmuenz@gmail.com|dmuenz@gmail.com>

When an object of my custom class is printed, I'd like to use
`deparse(substitute(x))` to refer to the object in a note. This works when I
explicitly type `print(my_object)`, but it fails if the object is auto-printed.

In the following example, note that when `my_object` is auto-printed, the
message is `"Run summary(x) to learn more"` rather than the desired `"Run
summary(my_object) to learn more"`.

```
my_object <- "Hello"
class(my_object) <- "custom_class"

print.custom_class <- function(x, ...) {
  input <- deparse(substitute(x))
  print(unclass(x))
  cat("Run summary(", input, ") to learn more\n", sep = "")
}

print(my_object)
#> [1] "Hello"
#> Run summary(my_object) to learn more

my_object
#> [1] "Hello"
#> Run summary(x) to learn more
```

In my real use case, I'm using the `cli` package in my print method to print a
note that contains a clickable link to actually run the code (at least in
RStudio). E.g.,

```
cli::cli_inform("Run {.run somepackage::foo({input})} to learn more")
```

But of course, this doesn't work when the object has been auto-printed.

I request that auto-printing be changed so that the print method can get the
original object name, either via `deparse(substitute(x))` or some other method.
Thanks for your consideration.

text:
*[Bug 18997] Allow print method for auto-printed object to see original object name*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18997>

Luke Tierney (<mailto:luke@stat.uiowa.edu|luke@stat.uiowa.edu>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |luke@stat.uiowa.edu
         Resolution|---                         |WONTFIX
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |CLOSED

--- Comment #1 from Luke Tierney (<mailto:luke@stat.uiowa.edu|luke@stat.uiowa.edu>) ---
It might in principle be useful to make available the most recently evaluated
expression (maybe as `.Last.expresion`) it would take some effort and I don't
think there is a strong enough case for that investment.

Also, I don't think it would address your issue. If the method used by
auto-printing could access and use the expression that produced your value you
would, for example, end up with

```
list(my_value)[[1]]
## [1] "Hello"
## Run summary(list(my_value)[[1]]) to learn more
```

I'm pretty sure that is not what you want.

Even with an explicit `print()` call your current approach would do things you
probably don't want, like

```
(function(z) print(z))(my_object)
## [1] "Hello"
## Run summary(z) to learn more
```

which doesn't make sense as the local variable `z` no longer exists.

You probably need to re-think your approach, keeping in mind that in R objects
do not have names, though they may be temporarily bound to variables.
